title: Separate status classification from next_action dispatch semantics
goal: Ensure the runtime implementation treats `status` as lifecycle classification and `next_action` as the sole canonical continuation dispatcher, without allowing status-derived heuristics or optional metadata to control continuation.
scope: Review all code paths that derive continuation behavior from transaction status.
Identify any places where `status` is used as an implicit dispatcher rather than a classifier.
Identify any dependencies on phase-style, step-style, or similarly optional metadata for deciding what to do next.
Define the target code-level contract so `next_action` drives continuation and `status` remains descriptive lifecycle state.
Update code paths as needed so valid canonical `next_action` takes precedence over status-derived branching.
Preserve explanatory uses of status for reporting, diagnostics, and operator understanding.
out_of_scope: Redesigning the canonical status set itself.
Rewriting persistence artifacts beyond what is required to preserve the `status` versus `next_action` contract.
inputs: Outputs from p0-t01, p0-t02, and p0-t03
Any runtime modules that compute or branch on transaction lifecycle state
Any helper or orchestration code that chooses follow-up actions
expected_outputs: A code-level mapping of all status-driven continuation branches.
A list of places where `next_action` already controls continuation correctly.
A list of places where `status` is incorrectly used as a dispatcher and must be corrected.
An implementation update that makes canonical `next_action` the authoritative continuation driver.
A short note describing the enforced division of responsibility between `status` and `next_action`.
implementation_notes_progress: Updated workflow guidance derivation so top-level `status` remains lifecycle classification while `next_action` remains the authoritative continuation directive.
Removed status-to-phase fallback in helper context loading paths so repo and commit helpers no longer derive continuation behavior from top-level status when active transaction phase is absent.
Adjusted success-response fallback behavior so canonical guidance fields are repopulated from the minimal continuation contract without requiring status-derived dispatch heuristics.
completion_notes: Reviewed workflow guidance and helper context loading paths that previously allowed status-derived fallback behavior to influence continuation handling.
Enforced the contract that `status` classifies lifecycle state while `next_action` remains the canonical continuation dispatcher.
Kept status-based reporting intact while removing dispatcher-style dependence on status and optional phase metadata.
implementation_notes: `status` must remain the canonical lifecycle classification field.
`next_action` must remain the canonical machine-readable continuation directive.
When valid canonical `next_action` is present, implementations must not override it with status-derived heuristics.
Status-specific 'typical next action' meanings in the plan are explanatory, not dispatch rules.
Optional phase-style or step-style metadata must not be required for continuation correctness.
acceptance_criteria: Every continuation code path that acts on transaction lifecycle has been reviewed for `status` versus `next_action` usage.
The implementation does not use `status` as the primary continuation dispatcher when valid canonical `next_action` is available.
The implementation does not require phase-style or step-style metadata to choose the next continuation step.
`status` remains available for lifecycle reporting and validation without redefining continuation control.
The resulting behavior matches the plan's model: `status` classifies, `next_action` dispatches.
review_checkpoints: Confirm that no valid `next_action` path is bypassed by a status-derived branch.
Confirm that status-based reporting still works after dispatcher cleanup.
Confirm that no checkpoint-state subfield is being used to silently replace top-level continuation control.
notes: This ticket is conceptually small but high leverage because ambiguous control flow here would propagate into resume, verify, commit, and terminal handling.
When reviewing code, prefer removal of status-driven dispatch heuristics over adding more explanatory metadata.
